longTailKeywords: how long does pink eye last

h1Title: Acute Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute conjunctivitis — universally known as pink eye — is inflammation of the transparent conjunctival membrane lining the inner eyelids and covering the white of the eye. It is one of the most common ocular diagnoses worldwide, accounting for approximately 1% of all primary care visits in high-income countries and affecting an estimated 6 million people in the United States annually. The condition presents with a red, discharging, often itchy eye. Viral causes (principally adenovirus) account for 65-80% of acute cases; bacterial causes (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae) account for 15-20%; and allergic triggers account for most remaining cases. Most cases resolve within 1-2 weeks without prescription treatment, though bacterial conjunctivitis shortens modestly with topical antibiotics.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 6 million US cases per year (Azari and Barney 2013); the leading cause of red eye in primary care, accounting for 1-2% of all GP consultations in the UK
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages; bacterial forms most common in children under 5; viral adenoviral conjunctivitis peaks in adults in institutional settings (schools, healthcare); allergic conjunctivitis most prevalent in atopic adults aged 20-40
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak; bacterial conjunctivitis most common in children aged 1-5; viral epidemic keratoconjunctivitis affects working-age adults in outbreaks
keyStats.globalCases: Trachoma (Chlamydia trachomatis conjunctivitis) remains a major cause of preventable blindness, affecting approximately 137 million people in 44 endemic countries as of WHO 2022 data

definition: Acute conjunctivitis (ICD-10: H10.30 — unspecified acute conjunctivitis) is inflammation of the bulbar and palpebral conjunctiva that develops acutely, typically over hours to a few days. The conjunctiva is a non-keratinised, highly vascular mucous membrane continuous from the inner surfaces of the eyelids (palpebral conjunctiva) to the limbus of the cornea (bulbar conjunctiva). Inflammation produces conjunctival vessel dilation (injection), increased vascular permeability causing chemosis (oedema), and a discharge varying from watery (viral, allergic) to mucopurulent or frankly purulent (bacterial). The acute form is distinguished from chronic conjunctivitis by symptom duration — typically under 4 weeks — and from keratoconjunctivitis by the absence of significant corneal involvement. The ICD-10 code H10.30 covers cases where the specific type (viral, bacterial, allergic, chlamydial) has not been clinically determined; subtypes have their own codes. Management is predominantly within primary care and general ophthalmology and is guided by the character of discharge, the presence of preauricular lymphadenopathy (suggesting viral or chlamydial cause), symptom severity, and the clinical setting. Gonococcal (Neisseria gonorrhoeae) and neonatal (ophthalmia neonatorum) conjunctivitis are treated as separate, urgent entities.

typesClassification.description: The most common type. Adenovirus serotypes 8, 11, 19, and 37 cause epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC), the most severe form, with watery discharge, preauricular lymphadenopathy, and potential corneal infiltrates. Highly contagious; spreads by direct contact and fomites. Self-limiting in 1-3 weeks.

Caused by Staphylococcus aureus (adults), Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, or Moraxella catarrhalis (children). Produces mucopurulent or purulent discharge with matted lids on waking. Responds to topical antibiotics but usually self-resolves in 7-14 days.

IgE-mediated seasonal (grass pollen, tree pollen) or perennial (house dust mite, pet dander) reaction to allergens. Bilateral intense itching is the hallmark; watery discharge and chemosis without purulent discharge.

Caused by Chlamydia trachomatis; presents as a chronic or subacute follicular conjunctivitis in sexually active adults from genital autoinoculation, or as ophthalmia neonatorum in newborns. Requires systemic antibiotic treatment.

Caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae; produces hyperacute onset with profuse purulent discharge within 12-24 hours, bilateral chemosis, and risk of corneal perforation. Requires urgent systemic antibiotics.

primarySymptoms: Conjunctival hyperaemia (redness) caused by dilation of bulbar and palpebral conjunctival vessels; typically diffuse and bilateral in viral and allergic forms, and sometimes unilateral initially in bacterial cases.

Discharge whose character indicates the likely cause: watery or serous in viral and allergic conjunctivitis; mucopurulent or thick yellow-green in bacterial conjunctivitis; hyperacute profuse pus in gonococcal infection.
Matted, crusted eyelids on waking in bacterial conjunctivitis — patients often describe their eye as 'glued shut' in the morning, distinguishing bacterial from viral or allergic forms.
Intense bilateral itching, the dominant symptom in allergic conjunctivitis, often rated the most bothersome feature; itching is mild or absent in bacterial and viral forms.
Foreign body sensation — a gritty, scratchy feeling in the eye present in most forms of conjunctivitis, ranging from mild (viral) to prominent (allergic, chlamydial).
Chemosis — swelling and boggy oedema of the conjunctival tissue, most pronounced in allergic and gonococcal conjunctivitis.
Preauricular lymphadenopathy (a tender nodule in front of the ear) is highly characteristic of adenoviral conjunctivitis and helps distinguish it from bacterial causes.
Photophobia, present in adenoviral epidemic keratoconjunctivitis when corneal infiltrates develop, and generally absent in uncomplicated bacterial or allergic conjunctivitis.
Vision remains normal or near-normal in most uncomplicated conjunctivitis; reduced acuity is a red flag suggesting corneal involvement or alternative diagnosis.
earlyWarningSigns: Unilateral red eye progressing to bilateral within 24-48 hours during an institutional outbreak (school, military, hospital) — suggests epidemic adenoviral keratoconjunctivitis, which is highly contagious.
Profuse purulent discharge accumulating within hours of onset in a sexually active adult — warrants same-day Neisseria gonorrhoeae swab and urgent treatment to prevent corneal perforation.
Follicular conjunctivitis persisting beyond 3 weeks with associated genitourinary symptoms — raises suspicion for chlamydial conjunctivitis requiring systemic treatment.
Newborn with eye discharge from day 2-5 of life — gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum is sight-threatening and requires urgent microbiological swab and systemic antibiotics.
emergencySigns: Hyperacute onset of profuse purulent discharge with severe conjunctival injection within 24 hours — gonococcal conjunctivitis, which can perforate the cornea within 24-72 hours without systemic antibiotic treatment.
Decreased visual acuity, severe photophobia, or pain on eye movement in the context of red eye — corneal involvement, herpes simplex keratitis, or acute iritis rather than simple conjunctivitis.
Red eye with acute eye pain, haloes around lights, and nausea — acute angle-closure glaucoma mimicking conjunctivitis; requires immediate tonometry.
Conjunctivitis in a contact lens wearer with corneal clouding or pain — bacterial keratitis, which can cause permanent vision loss within hours and requires emergency ophthalmological review.

causes.description: The most common infectious cause of acute conjunctivitis in adults. Spreads by direct contact with contaminated secretions, fomites (towels, equipment), and occasionally aerosol droplets. Adenovirus serotypes 8, 19, and 37 cause epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC), the most severe and contagious form; serotypes 3 and 7 cause pharyngoconjunctival fever. No specific antiviral treatment; highly contagious for 10-14 days.

causes.cause: Bacterial infection (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae)
causes.description: Bacterial conjunctivitis most commonly affects children; S. aureus predominates in adults. Infection reaches the conjunctiva from the ocular surface flora, direct inoculation by contaminated fingers, or respiratory spread. Mucopurulent discharge and morning lid crusting are the hallmarks. Most cases are self-limiting in 7-14 days; topical antibiotics shorten duration by 2-3 days.

Mast-cell degranulation triggered by allergens in atopic individuals releases histamine, prostaglandins, and leukotrienes, causing itching, redness, and chemosis. Seasonal allergens (tree pollen March-May, grass pollen June-September) cause seasonal allergic conjunctivitis, the most prevalent subtype. Perennial allergens (house dust mite, cat dander) cause year-round symptoms.

Serovars D-K cause inclusion conjunctivitis in sexually active adults via autoinoculation from genital secretions. Presents as a chronic follicular conjunctivitis with inferior palpebral follicles and a papillary reaction. Serovars A-C cause trachoma, the leading infectious cause of preventable blindness worldwide, through repeated infection producing cicatricial lid changes and corneal pannus.

HSV conjunctivitis presents as a unilateral follicular conjunctivitis, sometimes with vesicular skin lesions on the lid margin. The key danger is corneal involvement (dendritic ulcer); topical steroids must be avoided because they can worsen herpes simplex keratitis dramatically. Treatment is topical and systemic acyclovir.

causes.cause: Chemical, physical, and contact lens-related irritation
causes.description: Chlorinated pool water, cosmetics, contact lens solution preservatives (benzalkonium chloride), occupational chemical exposure, and excessive contact lens wear all cause chemical or toxic conjunctivitis. Contact lens wearers have a higher risk of bacterial conjunctivitis and bacterial keratitis from pathogen inoculation during lens handling.

riskFactors.factor: Close contact with an infected individual
riskFactors.description: Viral and bacterial conjunctivitis spread readily in households, schools, and daycare centres. Children in group childcare have 2-3 times the risk of bacterial conjunctivitis compared with those cared for at home.

Contact lens wearers have 7-10 times the risk of bacterial keratitis compared with spectacle wearers; even uncomplicated bacterial conjunctivitis is more severe and slower to resolve in contact lens wearers. Overnight lens wear multiplies risk further.

People with atopy have a 2-3 times higher prevalence of allergic conjunctivitis. The ocular surface mast cells are primed for IgE-mediated reaction. Approximately 15-40% of atopic individuals have significant seasonal allergic conjunctivitis.

Children in this age group have the highest incidence of bacterial conjunctivitis, related to immature immunity, eye-touching behaviour, and daycare exposure. Haemophilus influenzae and Streptococcus pneumoniae predominate.

Unprotected sexual contact with gonorrhoea or chlamydia raises the risk of ocular autoinoculation producing gonococcal or chlamydial conjunctivitis. Concurrent genitourinary symptoms should prompt conjunctival swab culture.

riskFactors.factor: Working in healthcare, schools, or military settings during outbreaks
riskFactors.description: Adenoviral epidemic keratoconjunctivitis attacks clusters and causes nosocomial outbreaks in ophthalmology clinics and general healthcare settings. Healthcare workers have occupational exposure risk.
HIV, transplant medication, or systemic steroid use predisposes to unusual or severe conjunctivitis caused by fungi, atypical bacteria, or herpes simplex/zoster reactivation.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 1-5 bear the highest incidence of bacterial conjunctivitis globally; H. influenzae and S. pneumoniae predominate in this group and the condition is often associated with concurrent otitis media.
Working-age adults in institutional settings (schools, military, healthcare) are the primary affected group during adenoviral EKC outbreaks, which can affect 10-40% of staff in a unit.
Atopic individuals aged 15-40 account for the largest proportion of allergic conjunctivitis; it co-occurs with hay fever in approximately 75% of affected individuals.

Trachoma (Chlamydia serovars A-C) disproportionately affects women and children in rural sub-Saharan Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia, accounting for 137 million people at risk of blindness (WHO 2022).
diagnosisOverview: Most acute conjunctivitis is diagnosed clinically without microbiological testing. History determines onset, laterality, discharge character, associated symptoms (itching for allergic, sore throat or URI for viral, genital symptoms for chlamydial), contact history, and contact lens use. The key clinical features that distinguish causes are: the character of discharge (watery = viral or allergic; mucopurulent = bacterial; hyperacute profuse pus = gonococcal); the presence of preauricular lymphadenopathy (viral — especially adenoviral); intense itching (allergic); papillae versus follicles on slit-lamp examination of the palpebral conjunctiva (follicles suggest viral or chlamydial; papillae suggest bacterial or allergic); and laterality (bilateral from onset in allergic and viral; unilateral onset in bacterial then spreading). Red flag features mandating ophthalmological review are: reduced visual acuity, moderate-to-severe photophobia, eye pain, corneal infiltrates, history of herpes simplex, contact lens wear, or immunosuppression. Conjunctival swab for bacterial culture and sensitivity is indicated for severe or refractory cases, neonates, suspected gonococcal or chlamydial cause, and contact lens wearers. Chlamydia PCR swab from the conjunctiva is the test of choice for suspected inclusion conjunctivitis. Slit-lamp examination differentiates follicular from papillary reactions and identifies corneal involvement. The differential includes acute iritis (photophobia, cells in anterior chamber, no discharge), acute angle-closure glaucoma (eye pain, haloes, high IOP), herpes simplex keratitis (unilateral, dendritic ulcer on fluorescein), and episcleritis.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination (discharge character, palpebral inspection, lymphadenopathy)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Primary diagnostic approach; distinguishes viral, bacterial, and allergic causes based on discharge type, follicles vs papillae, and associated features
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician examines both eyes with a light and possibly a slit-lamp. The inside of the lower eyelid is everted to inspect the conjunctival surface. No drops or instruments enter the eye. Takes 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Conjunctival swab for bacterial culture and sensitivity
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism and antibiotic susceptibilities in severe, recurrent, or atypical bacterial conjunctivitis and in neonatal or suspected gonococcal cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile cotton-tipped swab is gently rolled across the inner surface of the lower eyelid — a brief mild discomfort. Results take 2-3 days for culture; Gram stain result available within hours for urgent gonococcal cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Chlamydia PCR (conjunctival swab or nucleic acid amplification test)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses Chlamydia trachomatis inclusion conjunctivitis; far more sensitive than culture for this organism
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The same lower-lid swab technique as bacterial culture; the swab is placed in a specific transport medium for PCR analysis. Results typically in 24-48 hours. Genital NAAT testing is performed concurrently to guide systemic treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates follicular from papillary conjunctival reaction, detects corneal involvement (dendrites in herpes, subepithelial infiltrates in adenoviral EKC), and excludes anterior uveitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient places chin on a slit-lamp rest; the clinician directs a focused beam of light across the eye. Brief, bright light but not painful. Fluorescein drops may be added to detect epithelial defects. Takes 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies corneal epithelial defects — dendritic ulcers in herpes simplex, punctate epithelial erosions in adenoviral EKC, or contact-lens-related abrasions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single drop of orange fluorescein dye is instilled; the eye is examined under blue light. The dye washes out within minutes. Staining pattern identifies the type and location of any corneal damage.

diagnosticTests.test: Allergy skin-prick testing or serum specific IgE
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies specific allergens driving allergic conjunctivitis in cases where allergen avoidance or immunotherapy is being considered
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Skin-prick testing involves small drops of allergen extracts placed on the forearm, followed by a gentle prick. Results in 15-20 minutes. Blood test (RAST or ImmunoCAP) alternatively measures serum IgE to specific allergens; results in 3-5 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is guided by the underlying cause and severity. The 2013 NICE CKS guidelines and American Academy of Ophthalmology Preferred Practice Pattern both confirm that most acute conjunctivitis requires only supportive care. Viral conjunctivitis has no specific antiviral treatment (except herpes simplex, which requires topical and sometimes systemic acyclovir); management is symptomatic — cool compresses, lubricant artificial tears, and scrupulous hand hygiene to prevent spread. Antibiotic drops are not indicated for viral conjunctivitis. Bacterial conjunctivitis typically resolves spontaneously in 7-14 days even without antibiotics; topical antibiotics (chloramphenicol 0.5% drops or ointment, or fusidic acid 1% gel) shorten duration by approximately 2-3 days, increase the probability of clinical cure at day 5-6 by approximately 10-13% compared with placebo, and are reasonable for patient comfort and reducing transmission. In the UK, topical chloramphenicol is available over the counter. Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, ofloxacin) are reserved for severe bacterial cases, contact lens wearers, or resistant organisms. Gonococcal conjunctivitis requires urgent intramuscular or IV ceftriaxone 1 g plus topical saline irrigation and is treated as a medical emergency. Chlamydial inclusion conjunctivitis requires systemic azithromycin 1 g single dose or doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days; topical antibiotics alone are insufficient. Allergic conjunctivitis is managed with allergen avoidance, cool compresses, topical antihistamines (olopatadine, ketotifen), mast-cell stabilisers (sodium cromoglycate, nedocromil), or combined antihistamine-mast-cell stabiliser drops; topical corticosteroids are reserved for severe cases under ophthalmological supervision. Contact lens wearers must discontinue lenses until fully resolved.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical chloramphenicol 0.5% drops or 1% ointment
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antibiotic for bacterial conjunctivitis in the UK and many European countries; broad-spectrum against gram-positive and gram-negative organisms. Available over the counter. Instilled 2-hourly for 2 days then 4-hourly until 48 hours after clinical cure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Shortens duration by approximately 2-3 days; clinical cure rate by day 5-6 approximately 73% vs 58% with placebo (Sheikh et al. Cochrane 2012)

medicalTreatments.name: Topical fusidic acid 1% viscous drops (Fucithalmic)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative first-line topical antibiotic particularly effective against Staphylococcus aureus; twice-daily dosing improves adherence. Most commonly used in children.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent efficacy to chloramphenicol for staphylococcal conjunctivitis with better compliance due to twice-daily dosing

medicalTreatments.name: Topical olopatadine 0.1% or ketotifen 0.025% drops
medicalTreatments.description: Combined antihistamine and mast-cell stabiliser; the most effective topical agents for allergic conjunctivitis. Olopatadine 0.2% administered once daily is preferred for convenience. Provide rapid itch relief within minutes and sustained control with regular use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Superior to antihistamine alone; itch scores reduced by 60-70% vs placebo in randomised trials; symptom control maintained with twice-daily use throughout pollen season

medicalTreatments.name: Topical sodium cromoglycate 2% or nedocromil sodium 2%
medicalTreatments.description: Mast-cell stabilisers for allergic conjunctivitis; must be started 2-4 weeks before allergen season to be effective as prophylaxis. Used regularly 4 times daily during exposure periods.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce acute allergic responses by approximately 40-50% compared with placebo when used prophylactically; less effective as rescue treatment once symptoms established

medicalTreatments.name: IM ceftriaxone 1 g single dose (for gonococcal conjunctivitis)
medicalTreatments.description: Systemic antibiotic for the urgent treatment of Neisseria gonorrhoeae conjunctivitis. Topical antibiotics alone cannot eradicate gonococcal infection and are supplementary. Concurrent treatment for chlamydia with azithromycin 1 g is standard given high co-infection rates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of infection in over 95% of cases when combined with saline irrigation and contact tracing; delay increases corneal perforation risk

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic azithromycin 1 g single oral dose (for chlamydial conjunctivitis)
medicalTreatments.description: Treatment of choice for Chlamydia trachomatis inclusion conjunctivitis; targets the obligate intracellular organism systemically. Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days is an alternative. Partner notification and treatment are mandatory.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microbiological cure rates over 90% with single-dose azithromycin in genital chlamydia trials; topical-only treatment has under 50% cure rate in ocular chlamydia

alternativeTreatments.name: Cold compress application
alternativeTreatments.description: Cold moist compresses applied to closed eyelids for 10-15 minutes several times daily reduce conjunctival vessel dilation, chemosis, and itching in viral and allergic conjunctivitis. This supportive measure reduces symptom severity while the condition resolves. No controlled trial evidence for resolution speed, but widely recommended in clinical guidelines for symptom relief.

alternativeTreatments.name: Preservative-free lubricant eye drops
alternativeTreatments.description: Artificial tears without benzalkonium chloride preservative dilute and clear inflammatory mediators and allergens from the tear film, reducing irritation and foreign body sensation in viral, allergic, and chemical conjunctivitis. Some formulations can be refrigerated to enhance the cooling anti-inflammatory effect.

whySeeSpecialist: Most acute conjunctivitis resolves without specialist care. Ophthalmological referral is indicated for reduced visual acuity, moderate-to-severe photophobia, eye pain, corneal clouding, failure to respond to 5-7 days of topical antibiotics, suspected herpes simplex keratitis, contact lens-related keratitis, hyperacute purulent discharge, or recurrent episodes.
doctorSelectionGuide: For straightforward acute conjunctivitis, a GP, pharmacist, or urgent care clinician is appropriate. Ophthalmological review is needed when the cornea may be involved, when there is pain or photophobia, or when the diagnosis is uncertain. Seek an emergency ophthalmology service same day for suspected gonococcal conjunctivitis or any red flag feature.
hospitalCriteria: Emergency ophthalmology access for suspected gonococcal or herpes simplex keratitis cases
Microbiological swab and culture service for severe or refractory bacterial conjunctivitis
Slit-lamp and fluorescein examination capability for red flag cases
Chlamydia PCR testing for suspected inclusion conjunctivitis
keyFacilities: Community pharmacy with licensed chloramphenicol eye drops
General practice with basic ophthalmoscopy and slit-lamp access
Urgent eye care service or emergency eye unit for red flag cases
Genitourinary medicine clinic for concurrent chlamydial or gonococcal STI management
preventionStrategies: Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water for at least 20 seconds before and after touching the eyes or face, particularly in households with an infected person.
Avoid sharing face towels, pillows, washcloths, eye drops, or eye cosmetics with others.
Discard contact lenses, contact lens case, and any eye cosmetics used in the week before onset of bacterial or viral conjunctivitis.
Contact lens wearers should follow strict hand hygiene, replace lenses as directed, and never sleep in daily or extended-wear lenses beyond recommended periods.
Manage allergic triggers with antihistamine drops started before pollen season, regular bedding washing at 60°C for dust mite reduction, and pet dander avoidance.
Healthcare workers should follow contact precautions and eye protection during known adenoviral outbreaks in clinical settings.
lifestyleModifications: Stay home from school or work for 24-48 hours after beginning antibiotic treatment in bacterial conjunctivitis or until discharge has cleared, to avoid spreading infection.
Avoid touching or rubbing the eyes, which worsens inflammation and transfers infection to the other eye or to close contacts.
Remove and replace contact lenses only with clean hands, and switch to spectacles for the duration of the episode.
Apply cool compresses to the closed eyelids for 10-15 minutes several times daily to reduce redness, swelling, and itching.
Regularly clean and replace bedding pillowcases during viral or bacterial conjunctivitis to reduce reinfection.
Avoid swimming pools during active conjunctivitis and for 2 weeks after viral conjunctivitis resolves, as adenovirus persists in pool water.

recommended: Adequate vitamin A from orange and yellow vegetables (carrots, sweet potato, pumpkin) to maintain conjunctival and corneal epithelial health
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish or flaxseed — reduce ocular surface inflammation and improve tear film stability
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, peppers) — antioxidant support for mucosal immunity
Hydration with at least 1.5-2 L of water daily to support tear production
avoid: Allergen exposure for allergic conjunctivitis sufferers during high-pollen season (eating foods cross-reactive with pollen may worsen symptoms in oral allergy syndrome)
Rubbing eyes after eating foods that trigger allergic reactions (pollen-food allergy syndrome causes direct mucosal contact reactions)
Alcohol during antibiotic treatment as it may reduce mucosal immune defences

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate exercise is safe during uncomplicated conjunctivitis. Swimming should be avoided during active infection and for 2 weeks after viral conjunctivitis. Gym equipment touching (handles, machines) should be avoided during contagious phases, and hands washed thoroughly before and after any exercise. Contact sports should be avoided during active infection to prevent spread.
dailyManagement: Instil antibiotic drops as directed — use the pull-down-lower-lid technique, aim at the lower conjunctival sac, close the eye for 60 seconds after each drop, and use a clean tissue to wipe excess.
Remove discharge from crusted eyelids using a clean cloth or cotton pad soaked in cooled boiled water, wiping from inner to outer corner.
Wash hands every time you touch the eye or apply drops to prevent autoinoculation to the second eye.
Replace disposable contact lenses with new ones only after the infection has fully cleared; clean and disinfect reusable lenses before returning to use.
Return to GP or ophthalmologist if symptoms worsen after 5-7 days of treatment, if vision decreases, or if pain or photophobia develops.
prognosis: The prognosis for acute conjunctivitis is excellent in most cases. Viral conjunctivitis caused by adenovirus runs a self-limited course of 7-21 days; subepithelial corneal infiltrates from epidemic keratoconjunctivitis may persist for months causing photophobia and blurred vision but typically resolve without permanent damage. Bacterial conjunctivitis resolves in 7-14 days with or without antibiotics; topical antibiotics reduce duration by approximately 2-3 days. Allergic conjunctivitis is chronic and recurrent but does not cause permanent structural eye damage if managed appropriately. Chlamydial conjunctivitis treated systemically with azithromycin achieves over 90% microbiological cure and full visual recovery. Gonococcal conjunctivitis, if treated within 24-48 hours of onset, resolves without sequelae; delay risks corneal perforation and permanent visual impairment. Recurrent or untreated trachomatous chlamydial conjunctivitis in endemic areas progresses over years to trichiasis, corneal scarring, and preventable blindness — the leading infectious cause of blindness globally.
recoveryTimeline: Bacterial conjunctivitis without antibiotics: 7-14 days; with topical antibiotics: 5-7 days. Viral adenoviral conjunctivitis: 1-3 weeks for symptoms; subepithelial infiltrates may take months to clear. Allergic conjunctivitis: symptoms persist throughout exposure season; resolve within days of exposure cessation. Chlamydial inclusion conjunctivitis: microbiological cure within 2-4 weeks of systemic antibiotics; symptoms improve within 1-2 weeks.
complications: Corneal subepithelial infiltrates from adenoviral EKC causing persistent photophobia and blurred vision for weeks to months; most resolve without treatment but topical steroids under ophthalmological supervision may be needed.
Corneal perforation from untreated gonococcal conjunctivitis — occurs within 24-72 hours of onset without systemic antibiotics; a sight-threatening complication.
Pseudomembrane or true membrane formation on the palpebral conjunctiva in severe adenoviral or streptococcal conjunctivitis; requires careful peeling under local anaesthesia to prevent symblepharon.
Spread to otitis media in children with Haemophilus influenzae conjunctivitis — the conjunctivitis-otitis syndrome occurs in approximately 20-25% of paediatric bacterial cases.

Trachomatous trichiasis — repeated Chlamydia trachomatis infection in endemic settings causes cicatricial lid changes turning lashes inward, causing corneal abrasion, ulceration, and eventual blindness.
Neonatal blindness from untreated gonococcal or chlamydial ophthalmia neonatorum — prevented by prophylactic neonatal eye cleaning and povidone-iodine or antibiotic drops at birth.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Pink Eye Overview
supportResources.description: Clear patient-facing guide to conjunctivitis types, when to see a doctor, and treatment expectations from the AAO.

supportResources.name: NHS UK — Conjunctivitis
supportResources.description: Practical UK NHS guidance on pink eye symptoms, treatment, and when to seek professional help.

supportResources.name: CDC — Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye)
supportResources.description: US CDC guidance on infectious conjunctivitis, school exclusion policies, and outbreak control.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute iritis (anterior uveitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute iritis causes intense photophobia, deep aching eye pain, a small irregular pupil (miosis), and flare and cells in the anterior chamber on slit-lamp. Intraocular pressure is normal or low. Conjunctivitis produces a discharging eye without photophobia or anterior chamber cells, and rarely reduces vision. The two conditions have opposite management — iritis requires steroids, which worsen herpes simplex conjunctivitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute angle-closure glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with a painful red eye, but the hallmarks are very high intraocular pressure (40-70 mmHg), corneal oedema, haloes around lights, nausea, and a fixed mid-dilated pupil. There is no discharge, no itching, and vision is acutely reduced. Conjunctivitis produces discharge and preserved vision with normal IOP.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dry eye syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dry eye causes chronic bilateral burning, foreign body sensation, and paradoxical tearing from reflex hypersecretion; symptoms worsen in dry or windy environments and with screen use. There is no infectious discharge, no preauricular lymphadenopathy, and the Schirmer test is abnormal. Conjunctivitis is acute in onset with discharge and is often infectious or allergic in cause.

confusedWithConditions.name: Blepharitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Blepharitis is chronic inflammation of the eyelid margins with crusting, scaling, and lid-margin erythema; it causes chronic red eye and morning discharge but lacks the acute onset and volume of discharge seen in bacterial conjunctivitis. Slit-lamp shows lid-margin thickening, meibomian gland dysfunction, and collarettes around lash follicles rather than conjunctival follicles or papillae.

relatedConditions.name: Acute angle-closure glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Must be excluded in any acutely red eye; both present with redness but require fundamentally different emergency management.

relatedConditions.name: Acute iritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Key differential for painful red eye; often follows conjunctivitis or viral infection and requires opposite treatment (steroids vs antibiotics).

relatedConditions.name: Acute dacryocystitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Lacrimal sac infection produces medial canthal swelling, tearing, and discharge that can be confused with bacterial conjunctivitis.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes simplex infection
relatedConditions.relevance: HSV-1 can cause a follicular conjunctivitis that progresses to dendritic keratitis — a critical distinction because topical steroids given for presumed conjunctivitis can cause devastating corneal damage in herpes simplex keratitis.

faqs.question: What is acute conjunctivitis (pink eye)?
faqs.answer: Acute conjunctivitis is inflammation of the transparent membrane lining the inside of the eyelids and covering the white of the eye. It causes redness, discharge, and irritation. Viral causes account for 65-80% of cases, bacterial for 15-20%, and allergic triggers for most of the remainder. Most cases resolve within 1-2 weeks.

faqs.question: Is pink eye contagious?
faqs.answer: Viral and bacterial conjunctivitis are both contagious through direct contact with eye secretions, contaminated hands, and shared objects (towels, pillowcases). Viral adenoviral conjunctivitis is highly contagious for 10-14 days. Allergic conjunctivitis is not contagious. Strict hand hygiene and avoiding eye-touching reduce transmission significantly.

faqs.question: How long does pink eye last?
faqs.answer: Bacterial conjunctivitis typically resolves in 7-14 days without antibiotics; topical antibiotics shorten this to 5-7 days. Viral conjunctivitis lasts 1-3 weeks depending on the virus; adenoviral EKC can persist longer. Allergic conjunctivitis persists throughout allergen exposure. Most cases resolve completely without permanent eye damage.

faqs.question: Does pink eye always need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: No. Viral conjunctivitis does not respond to antibiotics. Bacterial conjunctivitis usually resolves in 7-14 days without treatment; topical antibiotics shorten this by 2-3 days. Antibiotics are recommended for severe bacterial cases, contact lens wearers, children, and anyone whose symptoms worsen after 5-7 days. Gonococcal conjunctivitis always requires urgent systemic antibiotics.

faqs.question: How can I tell if my conjunctivitis is viral or bacterial?
faqs.answer: Bacterial conjunctivitis typically causes mucopurulent (thick yellow-green) discharge and morning lid crusting. Viral conjunctivitis causes watery discharge with a recent cold or flu, preauricular lymphadenopathy, and often starts in one eye before spreading. Allergic conjunctivitis causes intense bilateral itching without purulent discharge. A clinician can distinguish these on examination.

faqs.question: Can I wear contact lenses with pink eye?
faqs.answer: No. Contact lens wear must stop immediately when conjunctivitis is diagnosed. Continuing to wear lenses worsens infection risk, slows recovery, and significantly increases the risk of serious bacterial keratitis. Discard the lenses and case used before onset. Switch to spectacles until the eye is fully clear for at least 24-48 hours.

faqs.question: What is the best eye drop for pink eye?
faqs.answer: For bacterial conjunctivitis, topical chloramphenicol 0.5% drops or fusidic acid 1% gel are first-line in the UK. For allergic conjunctivitis, olopatadine or ketotifen drops are most effective. Viral conjunctivitis has no specific drop — lubricant artificial tears provide comfort. Overuse of antibiotic drops for viral pink eye is ineffective and promotes resistance.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for pink eye?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if vision decreases, if there is moderate-to-severe pain or photophobia, if the cornea appears cloudy, if a newborn has eye discharge, if symptoms worsen after 5-7 days of treatment, or if you wear contact lenses. Sudden hyperacute profuse pus within 24 hours requires emergency evaluation for gonococcal conjunctivitis.

faqs.question: What causes pink eye in children?
faqs.answer: Bacterial conjunctivitis in children under 5 is most commonly caused by Haemophilus influenzae, Streptococcus pneumoniae, and Staphylococcus aureus. It frequently co-occurs with otitis media (ear infection). Viral conjunctivitis from adenovirus spreads rapidly in school and childcare settings. Neonatal conjunctivitis in newborns requires urgent evaluation to exclude gonorrhoea and chlamydia.

faqs.question: How do I prevent spreading pink eye to others?
faqs.answer: Wash hands frequently with soap and water. Avoid touching or rubbing the eyes. Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye cosmetics. Stay home from school or work until discharge has cleared or for 24-48 hours after starting antibiotics. Disinfect commonly touched surfaces. Avoid swimming pools during active infection.

faqs.question: Can pink eye cause permanent vision damage?
faqs.answer: Most cases of acute conjunctivitis cause no permanent vision damage. Complications are rare but include corneal infiltrates from adenoviral EKC, corneal perforation from untreated gonococcal conjunctivitis (within 24-72 hours), and corneal scarring from repeated trachomatous chlamydial infection in endemic regions. Timely treatment prevents nearly all serious outcomes.

faqs.question: What is allergic conjunctivitis?
faqs.answer: Allergic conjunctivitis is an IgE-mediated reaction of the conjunctival mast cells to airborne allergens such as pollen, house dust mite, or pet dander. It causes bilateral intense itching, watery discharge, and chemosis. It is not infectious and not contagious. Treatment includes allergen avoidance, antihistamine-mast-cell stabiliser eye drops, and, in severe cases, topical steroids.

faqs.question: Is gonococcal conjunctivitis serious?
faqs.answer: Yes. Gonococcal conjunctivitis is an ophthalmic emergency. Neisseria gonorrhoeae causes profuse purulent discharge within 12-24 hours of onset and can penetrate intact corneal epithelium, causing perforation within 24-72 hours without systemic antibiotics. It requires urgent intramuscular ceftriaxone, copious saline irrigation, and contact tracing.

faqs.question: Can adults get bacterial conjunctivitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bacterial conjunctivitis affects adults of all ages, with Staphylococcus aureus the most common organism in adults. Risk is increased in contact lens wearers, those with blepharitis or dry eye, and those in close contact with infected children. Most adults with bacterial conjunctivitis recover fully within 7-14 days with or without topical antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can conjunctivitis be a symptom of a systemic disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Conjunctivitis can accompany reactive arthritis (formerly Reiter syndrome), sarcoidosis, Kawasaki disease in children, rosacea, gonorrhoea, and chlamydia. Bilateral conjunctivitis with joint pain and urethritis in a young adult suggests reactive arthritis. HIV-associated conjunctivitis from unusual organisms may indicate immunosuppression. Persistent or recurrent conjunctivitis without a clear cause warrants systemic investigation.

metaDescription: Acute conjunctivitis (pink eye): bacterial, viral, and allergic causes explained. Symptoms, antibiotic treatment, contagion, and when to see a doctor reviewed.
